section.title: The List of Returned Exiles
section.sectionRationale: With the wall finished and the doors hung, Nehemiah turns from stone to people. He sets gatekeepers and guards, entrusts the city to his faithful, God-fearing brothers, and then, prompted by God, gathers the people to be enrolled. He finds the register of the first returnees and records it in full, the same list that stands in Ezra 2, so that the rebuilt city is filled with a people God knows by name. It is one movement: the city secured, and the recorded people of God brought home.
section.thesis: A wall is only as safe as the people within it are faithful, so Nehemiah guards the city and entrusts it to God-fearing men, then preserves the register of the returned exiles, showing that the God who brought a remnant home knows and names each one who belongs to Him.
section.frameNote: Seven beats: securing the city and its gates, verses 1 to 4; Nehemiah finds the register, verses 5 to 7; the men of the people, verses 8 to 38; the priests, Levites, and temple servants, verses 39 to 60; those who could not prove their lineage, verses 61 to 65; the totals and the freewill offerings, verses 66 to 72; and the settling of the people in their towns, verse 73.

units.label: Securing the city and its gates

verses.text: When the wall had been rebuilt and I had set the doors in place, the gatekeepers, singers, and Levites were appointed.
Then I put my brother Hanani in charge of Jerusalem, along with Hananiah the commander of the fortress, because he was a {{emet|faithful}} man who feared God more than most.

And I told them, “Do not open the gates of Jerusalem until the sun is hot. While the guards are on duty, keep the doors shut and securely fastened. And appoint the residents of Jerusalem as guards, some at their posts and some at their own homes.”
Now the city was large and spacious, but there were few people in it, and the houses had not yet been rebuilt.

units.label: Nehemiah finds the register
units.note: my God put it into my heart

verses.text: Then my God put it into my heart to assemble the nobles, the officials, and the people to be enrolled by {{yachas|genealogy}}. I found the genealogical register of those who had first returned, and I found the following written in it:
These are the people of the province who came up from the captivity of the exiles carried away to Babylon by Nebuchadnezzar its king. They returned to Jerusalem and Judah, each to his own town,
accompanied by Zerubbabel, Jeshua, Nehemiah, Azariah, Raamiah, Nahamani, Mordecai, Bilshan, Mispereth, Bigvai, Nehum, and Baanah. This is the count of the men of Israel:

units.label: The men of the people
units.note: family by family, town by town

verses.text: the descendants of Parosh, 2,172;
the descendants of Shephatiah, 372;
the descendants of Arah, 652;
the descendants of Pahath-moab (through the line of Jeshua and Joab), 2,818;
the descendants of Elam, 1,254;
the descendants of Zattu, 845;
the descendants of Zaccai, 760;
the descendants of Binnui, 648;
the descendants of Bebai, 628;
the descendants of Azgad, 2,322;
the descendants of Adonikam, 667;
the descendants of Bigvai, 2,067;
the descendants of Adin, 655;
the descendants of Ater (through Hezekiah), 98;
the descendants of Hashum, 328;
the descendants of Bezai, 324;
the descendants of Hariph, 112;
the descendants of Gibeon, 95;
the men of Bethlehem and Netophah, 188;
the men of Anathoth, 128;
the men of Beth-azmaveth, 42;
the men of Kiriath-jearim, Chephirah, and Beeroth, 743;
the men of Ramah and Geba, 621;
the men of Michmash, 122;
the men of Bethel and Ai, 123;
the men of the other Nebo, 52;
the descendants of the other Elam, 1,254;
the descendants of Harim, 320;
the men of Jericho, 345;
the men of Lod, Hadid, and Ono, 721;
and the descendants of Senaah, 3,930.

units.label: The priests, Levites, and temple servants
units.note: those set apart for the house of God

verses.text: The priests: the descendants of Jedaiah (through the house of Jeshua), 973;
the descendants of Immer, 1,052;
the descendants of Pashhur, 1,247;
and the descendants of Harim, 1,017.
The Levites: the descendants of Jeshua (through Kadmiel, through the line of Hodevah), 74.
The singers: the descendants of Asaph, 148.
The gatekeepers: the descendants of Shallum, the descendants of Ater, the descendants of Talmon, the descendants of Akkub, the descendants of Hatita, and the descendants of Shobai, 138 in all.
The temple servants: the descendants of Ziha, the descendants of Hasupha, the descendants of Tabbaoth,
the descendants of Keros, the descendants of Sia, the descendants of Padon,
the descendants of Lebanah, the descendants of Hagabah, the descendants of Shalmai,
the descendants of Hanan, the descendants of Giddel, the descendants of Gahar,
the descendants of Reaiah, the descendants of Rezin, the descendants of Nekoda,
the descendants of Gazzam, the descendants of Uzza, the descendants of Paseah,
the descendants of Besai, the descendants of Meunim, the descendants of Nephushesim,
the descendants of Bakbuk, the descendants of Hakupha, the descendants of Harhur,
the descendants of Bazlith, the descendants of Mehida, the descendants of Harsha,
the descendants of Barkos, the descendants of Sisera, the descendants of Temah,
the descendants of Neziah, and the descendants of Hatipha.
The descendants of the servants of Solomon: the descendants of Sotai, the descendants of Sophereth, the descendants of Perida,
the descendants of Jaala, the descendants of Darkon, the descendants of Giddel,
the descendants of Shephatiah, the descendants of Hattil, the descendants of Pochereth-hazzebaim, and the descendants of Amon.
The temple servants and descendants of the servants of Solomon numbered 392 in all.

units.label: Those who could not prove their lineage
units.note: excluded until their names could be found

verses.text: The following came up from Tel-melah, Tel-harsha, Cherub, Addon, and Immer, but they could not prove that their families were descended from Israel:
the descendants of Delaiah, the descendants of Tobiah, and the descendants of Nekoda, 642 in all.

And from among the priests: the descendants of Hobaiah, the descendants of Hakkoz, and the descendants of Barzillai (who had married a daughter of Barzillai the Gileadite and was called by their name).
These men searched for their family records, but they could not find them and so were excluded from the priesthood as unclean.
The governor ordered them not to eat the most holy things until there was a priest to consult the Urim and Thummim.

units.label: The totals and the freewill offerings
units.note: the whole assembly, and gifts given gladly

verses.text: The whole {{qahal|assembly}} numbered 42,360,
in addition to their 7,337 menservants and maidservants, as well as their 245 male and female singers.
They had 736 horses, 245 mules,
435 camels, and 6,720 donkeys.
Some of the heads of the families contributed to the project. The governor gave to the treasury 1,000 darics of gold, 50 bowls, and 530 priestly garments.
And some of the heads of the families gave to the treasury for the project 20,000 darics of gold and 2,200 minas of silver.
The rest of the people gave a total of 20,000 darics of gold, 2,000 minas of silver, and 67 priestly garments.

units.label: The people settle in their towns
units.note: each returned to his own place

verses.text: So the priests, Levites, gatekeepers, singers, and temple servants, along with some of the people and the rest of the Israelites, settled in their own towns. And by the seventh month the Israelites had settled in their towns.

emet.gloss: Truth, faithfulness, reliability. Hananiah is named a faithful man, one whose word and life can be trusted, and this is joined to his fearing God more than most. The city is entrusted not to the ablest soldier but to the most God-fearing man, for the security of God's people finally rests on faithful, reverent character.

yachas.gloss: To enroll or reckon by genealogy, to trace a line of descent. Nehemiah gathers the people to be registered by yachas and finds the written record of those who first returned. The register is not mere bookkeeping; it establishes who belongs to the covenant people, who may serve in the temple, and who inherits the promises.

qahal.gloss: Assembly, congregation, the gathered community of Israel. The whole qahal is numbered as one body brought home from exile. The same word describes Israel assembled before God at Sinai, and the Greek translation renders it ekklesia, the word the New Testament takes up for the church, the assembly of all whose names are known to God.

basic.context: The wall is finished and the doors are hung, so Nehemiah now cares for the people inside. He appoints gatekeepers and guards and hands the city over to two trustworthy, God-fearing men, giving careful orders for its safety. The city is large but nearly empty, so God moves Nehemiah to gather everyone to be registered, and he finds the record of the first exiles who came home under Zerubbabel, the same list that appears in Ezra 2. The long roll of families, priests, Levites, temple servants, and towns tells us that God did not bring back a nameless crowd; He brought back a people He knows one by one. Even those who could not prove their lineage are noted with care, and the whole assembly, having given freely for the work, settles into its towns.
basic.hermeneutics: Most of this chapter is a list, and a repeated one at that, so we read it as a careful record, not a set of commands. The point is not the individual numbers but what the register means: these are the recorded people of God, brought home by grace and known by name. Nehemiah preserves the list to fill the city with the right people and to root them in their covenant identity. The small differences from Ezra 2 are the ordinary marks of ancient record keeping and do not touch the meaning.
basic.originalLanguages: Hananiah is entrusted with the city because he is a man of emet, faithful and true, joined to a deep fear of God. Nehemiah gathers the people to be enrolled by yachas, reckoned by genealogy, and finds the written register that says who belongs. The whole qahal, the assembly of God's people, is then numbered as one body, a word that later becomes the church, the gathered people whose names God knows.
basic.typology: The register of those who returned points forward to the Lamb's book of life, the record of all who belong to God. Those excluded for want of a name in the book foreshadow the sober truth that only those written in heaven enter the city of God. Hananiah, the faithful man entrusted with the city, is a small picture of the faithful One to whom the true city is given.
basic.christ: This chapter quietly presses one question: is your name written among God's people? The excluded priests could not prove they belonged and were kept from the holy things until their names could be found. The gospel answers that question, for in Christ our names are written in the Lamb's book of life, not by our own lineage but by His grace. Jesus rejoices that the names of His own are written in heaven, and He is Himself the faithful One who secures and guards the city of God.
basic.god: God is the one who brings His people home and knows every one of them by name. He put it into Nehemiah's heart to gather and enroll them, moved the leaders to give freely, and preserved a record so that no true Israelite would be forgotten. He is faithful to His covenant, careful with His people, and worthy of the reverent trust that marks the men He raises up to guard His city.
basic.crossReferences: Ezra 2 records the same list of returnees. Revelation 21:27 says only those written in the Lamb's book of life enter the holy city. Luke 10:20 tells us to rejoice that our names are written in heaven. And Philippians 4:3 speaks of fellow workers whose names are in the book of life.
basic.oneStory: The God who carried a remnant into exile carries them home again and fills the rebuilt city with a people He knows by name. The register of the returned exiles, and the sober note of those who could not prove they belonged, point straight to the Lamb's book of life, in which the names of Christ's own are written forever, so that they may dwell secure in the city of God.

context.literary: The chapter is a hinge between the finished wall and the great assembly of chapter 8. It opens with a brief narrative of appointing guards and entrusting the city, then shifts into a long genealogical register that Nehemiah says he found and copied. The list is framed by Nehemiah's own words, my God put it into my heart and I found written in it, so that the roll of names is set within his first-person memoir. Reproducing the same list that stands in Ezra 2 ties the end of the wall-building to the beginning of the return, binding the whole restoration into one story.
context.historical: Jerusalem was large but underpopulated, and an empty city was a vulnerable one, which is why Nehemiah both posts guards and later, in chapter 11, resettles people inside the walls. Genealogical records mattered intensely in the post-exilic community: they determined who could claim covenant membership, who could hold priestly office, and who had rights to ancestral land. The Urim and Thummim were the priestly means of seeking God's decision, here longed for but not presently available, marking the humility of the restored community.
context.canonical: The register echoes Ezra 2 almost exactly and belongs to the same restoration history that Ezra-Nehemiah tells as one work. Its concern with recorded names runs through Scripture, from the census lists of Numbers to the book of life in Exodus 32, the Psalms, Daniel, and finally Revelation. The gathered assembly, the qahal, is the Old Testament seed of the New Testament ekklesia, the church, so that this dusty list stands in a line that reaches to the countless multitude no one can number, gathered before the throne.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the rebuilt city being filled with the recorded, covenant people of God, entrusted to faithful and God-fearing leaders, so that the restoration is not merely of walls but of a people who belong to the LORD and are known by name.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes how the post-exilic community was secured and enrolled; it does not prescribe genealogical registers or gate-keeping customs for the church today. What endures is the truth that God knows His people by name, that leadership should be entrusted to the faithful and God-fearing, and that belonging to God's people is a matter of being recorded by grace, fulfilled in the Lamb's book of life.

debates.question: Why does Nehemiah repeat the register already given in Ezra 2, and why do some of the numbers differ?

views.label: One list, preserved for a new purpose
views.case: Nehemiah found and copied the original register of the first return in order to repopulate and re-establish the covenant community; reproducing it here roots the finished wall in the identity of the people who first came home, and the differences are incidental to that purpose.

views.label: Ordinary variation in ancient transmission
views.case: The small differences in names and totals are the expected marks of copying numbers and lists by hand over time, or of counting at slightly different moments; the substance of the register, the families and their belonging, is unchanged, so the variations carry no theological weight.

debates.bearing: Either way the repeated list underscores that the same people God first brought home are the people who now fill the city; the point is covenant belonging, not arithmetical precision.

debates.question: What are we to make of those excluded from the priesthood for lacking genealogical proof?

views.label: A necessary guarding of holiness
views.case: The priesthood and the most holy things had to be protected from those who could not establish their claim; the exclusion is provisional, not final, since the governor withholds judgment until a priest can consult the Urim and Thummim, leaving the door open should their record be found.

views.label: A signpost to the necessity of being recorded
views.case: The scene dramatizes that belonging cannot rest on assumption but must be written and known; it foreshadows the sober truth that only those whose names are in the Lamb's book of life may enter the holy city, and points to the need for a surer register than human records can provide.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge on the gospel: human genealogies fail and exclude, but in Christ our names are written in heaven by grace, securing a belonging no lost record can undo.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on a few weighty words. The city is handed to Hananiah because he is a man of emet, faithful and dependable, and this faithfulness is bound to his fearing God more than most, so that trustworthiness and reverence are named as the qualifications for guarding God's people. Then, moved by God, Nehemiah enrolls the community by yachas, genealogical reckoning, and recovers the written register of the first return; this is the language of belonging, for to be enrolled is to be counted among the covenant people, to have a claim to serve at the temple and to inherit the promises. Finally the whole company is gathered as one qahal, the assembly of Israel, the same word used of the congregation gathered before God at Sinai and rendered in Greek as ekklesia, the church. Faithfulness, enrollment, assembly: the vocabulary of a people secured, recorded, and gathered by their God.

typology.type: The register of the returned exiles, each name recorded
typology.fulfillment: The Lamb's book of life, in which the names of all who belong to God are written, Revelation 21:27 and Luke 10:20.

typology.type: Those excluded for lack of a name in the record
typology.fulfillment: The sober truth that only those written in the book of life enter the city of God, Revelation 20:15 and 21:27.

typology.type: Hananiah the faithful, God-fearing man entrusted with the city
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the faithful One to whom the true and lasting city is given, and who guards His people, Hebrews 3:5-6 and Revelation 21:2.

study.christ: Beneath the long roll of names this chapter asks whether a person truly belongs to God, and it answers with sober honesty. Some who came up from exile could not prove their descent, and certain priests, unable to find their names in the records, were kept from the holy things until their claim could be established. Human genealogies, it turns out, can fail and exclude. The gospel meets that fear head on, for Christ writes the names of His own in the Lamb's book of life, not by bloodline or achievement but by grace, so that Jesus tells His disciples to rejoice above all that their names are written in heaven. He is the faithful One, greater than Hananiah, to whom the everlasting city is entrusted, and He guards it so that nothing unclean and no one whose name is missing can enter, while all whom He has recorded dwell there secure. The register of the returnees is a shadow; the substance is the roll of the redeemed, written in the blood of the Lamb.
study.god: God is revealed as the faithful keeper of His people. He does not save a nameless mass but a company He knows one by one, and He preserves their record so that none who belong are forgotten. It was God who put it into Nehemiah's heart to gather and enroll them, God who had first stirred them to return, and God who moved the leaders to give freely for His house. He entrusts His city to those who fear Him, He guards the holiness of His worship, and He holds the future of the seemingly forgotten. This is a God of covenant faithfulness, careful and personal, who writes down the names of His own and will not lose a single one.

crossReferences.note: The parallel register of the same returned exiles, recorded at the time of the first return under Zerubbabel, with minor differences in names and numbers.
Moses and the LORD speak of being blotted out of or written in God's book, the covenant record of His people that stands behind the book of life.
Jesus tells His disciples to rejoice that their names are written in heaven, the true register of those who belong to God.
Only those written in the Lamb's book of life enter the holy city, the fulfillment of the recorded, guarded people pictured here.

study.oneStory: The God who sent a remnant into exile brings them home again and fills the rebuilt city with a people He knows by name, entrusting it to the faithful and recording every family in the register of the return. The roll of names, and the sober note of those who could not prove they belonged, point straight to the Lamb's book of life, in which Christ writes the names of His own by grace. The whole restoration presses toward the city of God, secured and guarded, filled at last with the countless multitude whose names are known and kept forever.

quiz.q: Why did Nehemiah entrust Jerusalem to Hananiah?

options.text: Because he was a faithful man who feared God more than most.
options.note: Verse 2 names his faithfulness and fear of God as the reason he was trusted with the city.

options.text: Because he was the strongest soldier in the army.
options.note: Hananiah commanded the fortress, but the reason given is his faithful, God-fearing character, not military strength.

options.text: Because he was the wealthiest man in Jerusalem.
options.note: Wealth is not mentioned; the text points to faithfulness and the fear of God.

options.text: Because he had rebuilt the wall himself.
options.note: The wall was the work of many; Hananiah is trusted for his character, not for building it.

quiz.q: What did Nehemiah find when God moved him to assemble the people?

options.text: The genealogical register of those who had first returned from exile.
options.note: Verse 5 records that he found the register of the first returnees and copied it.

options.text: A hidden store of gold and silver in the temple.
options.note: Offerings are mentioned later, but what he found was the genealogical register, not hidden treasure.

options.text: A new law that Ezra had written.
options.note: The reading of the Law comes in chapter 8; here Nehemiah finds the register of returnees.

options.text: A letter from the king of Persia.
options.note: No royal letter appears here; he found the record of those who had returned.

quiz.q: What happened to those who could not prove their descent from Israel?

options.text: The priests among them were excluded from holy things until their claim could be established.
options.note: Verses 63 to 65 show the priests without records kept from the most holy things pending the Urim and Thummim.

options.text: They were immediately put to death.
options.note: They were excluded and their case deferred, not executed.

options.text: They were sent back to Babylon.
options.note: They remained with the people; only their priestly claim was withheld.

options.text: They were made rulers of the city.
options.note: The opposite: their claim was in question, so they were held back, not exalted.

quiz.q: Why does Nehemiah record this long list of names and numbers?

options.text: To show that God brought home a recorded, covenant people whom He knows by name, not a nameless crowd.
options.note: The register establishes covenant belonging and fills the city with the right people.

options.text: To boast about how large the returning army was.
options.note: The numbers are modest and the aim is belonging and identity, not military boasting.

options.text: To calculate how much tax each family owed.
options.note: The list is a genealogical register of belonging, not a tax roll.

options.text: Simply to fill space in the book.
options.note: The record is purposeful, marking who belongs to God's restored people.

quiz.q: How does the register of returnees point forward to Christ?

options.text: It foreshadows the Lamb's book of life, in which Christ writes the names of His own by grace.
options.note: The recorded people anticipate those whose names are written in heaven, Luke 10:20 and Revelation 21:27.

options.text: It proves that only those with pure bloodlines can be saved.
options.note: The gospel shows belonging comes by grace in Christ, not by lineage; the list points beyond itself.

options.text: It teaches that names and records do not matter to God.
options.note: The opposite: the care taken with names shows how fully God knows His people.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ at all.
options.note: The recorded, guarded people point directly to the Lamb's book of life and the city of God.

questions: Nehemiah entrusted the city to men marked by faithfulness and the fear of God rather than by talent alone. What are you looking for when you decide whom to trust, and what does God look for?
God brought home a people He knew name by name, not a faceless crowd. How does it change your week to know that God knows you personally and has not forgotten you?

The city was large but nearly empty, and Nehemiah acted to fill it with the right people. Where might God be calling you to invest in the community of His people rather than leaving it thin and vulnerable?

Some could not prove they belonged and were held back from the holy things. How does the assurance that your name is written in the Lamb's book of life, by grace and not by record, steady you before God?
The leaders gave freely for the work of God's house. Where is God inviting you to give gladly, out of gratitude for being brought home and counted among His people?

translationNotes.note: This register runs closely parallel to Ezra 2:1-70, with minor differences in some names and numbers; these are the ordinary marks of ancient record keeping and do not affect the meaning, that these are the covenant people brought home from exile.
Temple servants renders the Hebrew Nethinim, those given to assist the Levites in the service of the temple; the term recurs in verses 60 and 73.

The Urim and Thummim, literally Lights and Perfections, were the priestly means of seeking God's decision; the community awaits a priest able to consult them, marking their humble, in-between situation.

A daric was a Persian gold coin and a mina a unit of weight for silver; the BSB footnotes give approximate modern equivalents, but the point is the freely given generosity of the people for God's house.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Nehemiah 7:1-73, all 73 verses included in order. Segments concatenated with single spaces; noteId, lineBreak, and heading markers ignored. The chapter contains no em or en dashes; hyphens appear only within proper names (for example Pahath-moab, Beth-azmaveth, Kiriath-jearim, Tel-melah) and are retained as correct spellings. Verse 68 is included, following the BSB, which notes it is present in some Hebrew manuscripts and in Ezra 2:66. The register parallels Ezra 2 with minor numerical and name variations, noted in translationNotes.
